patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead
[PATCH v2] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead
[PATCH v3] btrfs: release extent lock per folio in readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Btrfs file system's readahead behavior to release extent locks per folio, rather than holding them for the entire range. This change is intended to prevent a race condition between readahead and direct IO writes, which can cause tasks to hang on the extent lock. The problem arises when readahead fails due to memory allocation issues, leaving pages in an inconsistent state and causing subsequent reads to block on the extent lock. By releasing locks per folio, the patch aims to alleviate this issue and reduce stress on the system.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not address a potential issue where multiple tasks can block on each other's extent locks, and suggested adding a lock to prevent this

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 2/2] virtio-balloon: add stats push mode
Re: [RFC PATCH 2/2] virtio-balloon: add stats push mode
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch adds a stats push mode to virtio-balloon, but concerns were raised about potential blocking operations.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about potential blocking operations in the virtio-balloon stats push mode, suggesting that the issue might be related to CH's interfaces or a misunderstanding of the interaction.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 05:52:29PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 6/16/26 16:44, Gregory Price wrote:
> > That makes sense, although don't you just push the blocking operation
> > into yet another thread on the host?
> 
> I think timers are run from the QEMU main thread, so no separate thread just for
> the timer.
> 
> And IIRC, there will be no blocking. At least if I understand your concern
> correctly.
> 
> balloon_stats_poll_cb() will do a virtqueue_push()+virtio_notify(), which will
> notify the device. The main thread will continue afterwards doing what a main
> thread usually does.
> 
> A VCPU will process the request in the VM and send it back + notify the device.
>

Entirely possible I just bungled the interaction then and/or CH's
interfaces introduce a blocking op that shouldn't.

Thanks for the feedback, we can probably drop this patch.  Unless
there's any particular pushback for 1/2, should i leave as-is or
resubmit separately w/o RFC?

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Clarified that timers are run from the QEMU main thread and that there should be no blocking operations in the virtio-balloon stats push mode.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the potential issue and considered revising or withdrawing the patch, but asked if there was any particular pushback for the first part of the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 05:52:29PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 6/16/26 16:44, Gregory Price wrote:
> > That makes sense, although don't you just push the blocking operation
> > into yet another thread on the host?
> 
> I think timers are run from the QEMU main thread, so no separate thread just for
> the timer.
> 
> And IIRC, there will be no blocking. At least if I understand your concern
> correctly.
> 
> balloon_stats_poll_cb() will do a virtqueue_push()+virtio_notify(), which will
> notify the device. The main thread will continue afterwards doing what a main
> thread usually does.
> 
> A VCPU will process the request in the VM and send it back + notify the device.
>

Entirely possible I just bungled the interaction then and/or CH's
interfaces introduce a blocking op that shouldn't.

Thanks for the feedback, we can probably drop this patch.  Unless
there's any particular pushback for 1/2, should i leave as-is or
resubmit separately w/o RFC?

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 2/2] virtio-balloon: add stats push mode
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: virtio-balloon patch for stats push mode, discussion around latency and thread efficiency

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about latency and thread efficiency in virtio-balloon stats push mode, suggesting a timer-based approach like QEMU's. Identified potential issues with blocked threads and reaping guests.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 04:32:46PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 6/16/26 15:57, Gregory Price wrote:
> > 
> > Definitely an RFC here because I'm not sure if I was missing something
> > that might help me solve the problem.
> 
> Well, in QEMU we just run a timer internally that does the polling.
> 
> Then, upper layers in the stack can ask QEMU for the latest stats.
> 
> There, you just get the stats along with a "last-update" timestamp.
>

That makes sense, although don't you just push the blocking operation
into yet another thread on the host?

Assuming it's not cancel-able, there's a blocked thread there you have
to reap.  Vs the guest being unresponsive and not sending updates, you
just reap the whole guest or take some other corrective action.

I suppose to the orchestrator reaping QEMU and reaping the guest looks
the same.  The difference is just where the thread lives, hmmmm

I'll make a note to inspect QEMU's solution to see if that's handled
or if it would be subject to the same issue.

Thanks!
~Gregory

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Provided an alternative solution using timers and virtqueue_push() to avoid blocking operations. Clarified the interaction between QEMU's main thread and virtio-balloon.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged David's feedback and suggested dropping the patch unless there's significant pushback. Asked about resubmitting without RFC.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 05:52:29PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 6/16/26 16:44, Gregory Price wrote:
> > That makes sense, although don't you just push the blocking operation
> > into yet another thread on the host?
> 
> I think timers are run from the QEMU main thread, so no separate thread just for
> the timer.
> 
> And IIRC, there will be no blocking. At least if I understand your concern
> correctly.
> 
> balloon_stats_poll_cb() will do a virtqueue_push()+virtio_notify(), which will
> notify the device. The main thread will continue afterwards doing what a main
> thread usually does.
> 
> A VCPU will process the request in the VM and send it back + notify the device.
>

Entirely possible I just bungled the interaction then and/or CH's
interfaces introduce a blocking op that shouldn't.

Thanks for the feedback, we can probably drop this patch.  Unless
there's any particular pushback for 1/2, should i leave as-is or
resubmit separately w/o RFC?

~Gregory

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH 1/2] virtio-balloon: extend stats with memory composition and pressure data
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch extends virtio-balloon stats to include memory composition and pressure data.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised concern about the risk of features being removed and suggested it's unlikely to be fixed.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Clarified that some of the features are already exported through /proc/vmstat and others through /proc/pressure, indicating that the patch is mostly fine.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [Lsf-pc] [LSF/MM/BPF TOPIC][RFC PATCH v4 00/27] Private Memory Nodes (w/ Compressed RAM)
Re: [PATCH v2] cgroup/cpuset: rebind mm mempolicy to effective_mems, not mems_allowed
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to rebind mm mempolicy to effective_mems instead of mems_allowed in cgroup/cpuset.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price
review_comments.summary: Raised questions about the user-visible implications of the change, suggesting that the two values should be equivalent due to the hotplug lock.

review_comments.author: Waiman Long
review_comments.summary: Clarified that effective_mems and guarantee_online_mems() are equivalent for v2, but not for v1, providing an example of the differing behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/16/26 9:44 AM, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 08:59:07AM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
>> On 6/16/26 05:43, Waiman Long wrote:
>>> On 6/15/26 10:26 PM, Waiman Long wrote:
>>>>
>>>> The reason why I am suggesting to use cs->effective_mems to keep the old
>>>> cgroup v1 behavior. If the consensus is to use the output of
>>>> guarantee_online_mems() for mpol_rebind_mm(), I will not be against that but
>>>> it will be a slight change in user-visible behavior.
> I'm not grok'ing what is user-visible here.
>
> The two values should effectively be equivalent because we're
> using this value to constrain mpol's during a hotplug event.
>
> If the values differed, you would be saying there's a race condition
> that could affect correctness of the rebind (which can't happen,
> because this whole thing is done under the hotplug lock btw).
>
> Can you help me understand?

The effective_mems and guarantee_online_mems() should be more or less 
equivalent for v2, but not for v1. In my response to David, I gave an 
example of how the behavior will differ.

Cheers,
Longman
